title: Welsh cakes
subtitle: Spiced currant griddle cakes cooked on a bakestone
excerpt: Welsh cakes (picau ar y maen) are cooked on a griddle or heavy frying pan rather than baked in an oven. A spiced shortcrust enriched with egg and currants, cut into rounds and cooked on both sides until pale gold outside and soft within.
sourceNotes: Welsh cakes (picau ar y maen, literally cakes on the stone) are a traditional Welsh griddle cake documented in Welsh household cookery from the 19th century. S. Minwel Tibbott, Welsh Fare (1976), drawing on older sources, records the griddle cake as a standard Welsh household bake. The combination of spiced shortcrust with currants, cooked on a bakestone (maen), predates oven baking in Welsh homes. Florence White, Good Things in England (1932), also records the tradition.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Welsh cakes are best eaten warm on the day. Reheat briefly in a dry frying pan over low heat.

glossaryTerms.definition: Welsh cakes need a medium heat: hot enough to colour the surface within a couple of minutes, but cool enough for the inside to cook through before the outside burns. A bakestone or heavy cast iron frying pan holds heat evenly. Test by placing a small scrap of dough in the dry pan; it should sizzle gently and colour in about 90 seconds on each side.

content.text: Welsh cakes are cooked on a dry griddle or heavy frying pan without any fat in the pan. The dough is a lightly enriched shortcrust, similar to a scone but drier and less liquid. The
is everything; too hot and the outside scorches before the inside cooks. A cast iron pan or a bakestone gives the most even heat.

Sift the flour, baking powder, mixed spice, salt, and sugar into a bowl. Rub in the cold butter until the mixture resembles coarse breadcrumbs. Stir in the currants. Add the beaten egg and mix to a firm dough; add a tablespoon of milk only if the dough will not come together. Turn out and knead briefly into a smooth ball.

Roll the dough on a lightly floured surface to 7 mm thick. Cut rounds with a 6 cm plain cutter. Heat a heavy frying pan or bakestone over medium heat with no fat. Cook the welsh cakes in batches of 4 to 6, for 2 to 3 minutes per side, until pale golden brown on both sides and cooked through — the sides should look dry, not raw. Do not rush with high heat; the inside needs time to cook. Transfer to a rack and dust generously with caster sugar while still warm.
